items.myth: Tight visual clusters prove the data has that many natural groups.
items.reality: Projection and algorithm choice create layout; density in 2D does not establish optimal k or generative cluster structure without quantitative validation.
items.example: t-SNE with different perplexity can split one blob into two islands—k-means silhouette may still favor k=1 in the original space.

items.myth: If classes overlap in the embedding plot, the model or representation is useless.
items.reality: Overlap can mean linear/non-linear separation lives in other dimensions, classes are genuinely similar, or the projection lost separating directions.
items.example: MNIST 4 vs 9 overlap in 2D UMAP while a classifier still achieves high accuracy using all pixel dimensions.

items.myth: A prettier plot with more separated colors means a better embedding method.
items.reality: Dramatic separation can reflect aggressive non-linear stretching or overfitting to parameters—not downstream task utility.
items.example: Extreme t-SNE perplexity can fragment a single class into faux clusters that hurt kNN label transfer.

items.myth: 3D plots always reveal structure hidden in 2D.
items.reality: Adding a dimension adds occlusion and viewing-angle bias; interactivity helps but does not guarantee new separability.
items.example: Rotating a 3D PCA view may separate points along PC3 that were already available as a 1D color encoding on a 2D plot.
